html:
COLOPL, Inc. (TSE:3668) Thesis

COLOPL at 377 yen is not the Applibot or Disney Pixel RPG story in the prior draft. That was wrong. The real thesis is cleaner but less explosive: COLOPL is a disliked Japanese mobile-game cash box whose market cap is now below its net liquid financial assets, while the core game business has just crossed back into quarterly profitability after a cost reset.

The market is labeling it as a declining gacha developer. That label is partly earned: legacy titles are aging, FY2026 H1 sales fell 28%, management gives no guidance, and the company has spent too much capital on low-return optionality like Web3. But the current price is already close to liquidation logic. At the latest close of 377 yen, market cap is about 48.5 billion yen. The March 2026 balance sheet shows 46.9 billion yen of cash, 9.1 billion yen of operational investment securities, 7.9 billion yen of investment securities, and only 5.4 billion yen of total liabilities. Net liquid financial assets are roughly 58.5 billion yen, or about 455 yen per share, versus the stock at 377 yen.

That means investors are paying less than net liquid assets and getting the operating business, the Dragon Quest Walk revenue-share stream, the MAGES console/adventure-game slate, the venture portfolio, and five mobile-game pipeline shots for free. This is not enough by itself if management keeps hoarding cash, but it is a real margin of safety.

The important inflection is Q2. H1 numbers looked bad because investment exits lapped a strong prior period, but Q2 standalone revenue improved sequentially to about 5.3 billion yen and operating profit was roughly 0.6 billion yen after a Q1 operating loss. The entertainment segment did better: Q2 segment sales were about 5.1 billion yen and operating profit about 0.7 billion yen. Management explicitly attributes the improvement to lower advertising and cost control while Dragon Quest Walk continued contributing. The January 2026 career transition program removed 104 employees, above the planned 70, with a 273 million yen one-time charge already booked. That should make the second-half cost base structurally lighter.

The forward valuation is therefore asymmetric. On headline earnings, COLOPL does not screen cheap: H1 EPS was only 6.42 yen and TTM earnings are depressed. But the right frame is current-cycle asset value plus forward normalized earnings. If the post-restructuring entertainment business can sustain even 2.0-3.0 billion yen of annual operating profit, the entire operating business is being valued at less than zero after net liquid assets. If a single pipeline title works, the earnings torque is large because the starting enterprise value is negative.

The pipeline is not empty. COLOPL says it has five mobile games in development, including two location-based titles, the one category where the company has genuine differentiated know-how through Dragon Quest Walk. It also lists ten PC/console projects, with STEINS;GATE RE:BOOT scheduled for August 20, 2026 across six platforms. KAZUMA KANEKO'S Tsukuyomi launched in April. These are not guaranteed blockbusters, but at today’s price the stock does not need a blockbuster. It needs one or two titles to prove the company is not in terminal runoff.

The variant perception is that COLOPL is no longer a growth stock and not yet treated as a capital-return stock. That creates the opening. The shares fell sharply after Q2 and are near a 52-week low, while the balance sheet still supports the equity and the 20 yen FY2025 dividend implies a 5.3% yield if maintained. Retail sentiment in Japan appears poor, and that matters because this is a small, ignored name without obvious institutional sponsorship. Any shift from “dead mobile-game company” to “asset-backed, profitable, shareholder-yielding option book” can move the multiple fast.

The biggest weakness is capital allocation. There is no large buyback in the current filings, treasury shares barely changed, and the 2026 dividend is not committed. Founder Naruatsu Baba still owns about 48%, so minority holders depend on governance and discipline. The Web3 effort is a warning, not a bull point: Brilliantcrypto’s token has collapsed from its launch levels and should be valued near zero unless proven otherwise. The investment portfolio is also volatile and has already produced both gains and impairments.

Still, the downside is unusually contained for a game stock. Cash minus all liabilities is roughly 323 yen per share. Net current assets minus all liabilities are roughly 431 yen per share. Book value is about 518 yen per share. A stock at 377 yen is already discounting a lot of future disappointment.

Bear case: 300 yen, down 20%. Dragon Quest Walk decays faster, new titles fail, the 20 yen dividend is cut, investment securities are written down, and management keeps spending on speculative projects. The stock trades at roughly 0.55-0.60x stressed book despite the balance sheet.

Base case: 580 yen, up 54%. The post-restructuring business stabilizes at 2-3 billion yen of annual operating profit, the company maintains a meaningful dividend, and investors value the stock closer to net liquid assets plus a modest value for the operating platform. This is still only around 1.1x current book value.

Bull case: 900 yen, up 139%. One location-game or IP-backed title hits, Dragon Quest Walk remains durable, console/adventure releases contribute, and management adds a buyback or clearer payout policy. The market then values COLOPL as net liquid assets plus a real profitable game pipeline rather than a melting cash box.

At 377 yen, COLOPL is not a perfect compounder. It is an asset-backed call option on a cost-reset Japanese game company where the market is paying less than liquid financial assets and assigning negative value to any future hit. The upside depends on management proving that the cash belongs to shareholders and that the pipeline is not just expense. The current price gives enough protection to underwrite that uncertainty.
